title: Garlic Butter Salmon Pasta
subtitle: A pasta dish for any night of the week.
excerpt: Garlic Butter Salmon Pasta. A weeknight pasta — sauce in one pan, pasta in another, finished together.
sourceNotes: From Rebecca's personal cookbook — a recipe she's made many times in her own kitchen.

content.text: Garlic Butter Salmon Pasta. A British home-cook's dish — the kind of recipe families adjust across generations. Adjustable for what you need.
Pasta in a pan, sauce in another. The trick is the meeting — the noodles finish in the sauce, holding the starchy water that binds the two.

Bring a large pot of generously salted water to a rolling boil. Cook the fettuccine according to the package directions until al dente (usually about 10-12 minutes). Before draining, scoop out about 1/2 cup of the starchy pasta water and set aside - this is liquid gold if your sauce needs thinning later. Drain the pasta but don't rinse it.

While the pasta is cooking, pat your salmon pieces dry with paper towels (this helps them get crispy). Season generously with salt and pepper on all sides. Heat the olive oil in a large skillet over medium-high heat until shimmering. Carefully add the salmon pieces and cook for about 2-3 minutes per side until they develop a beautiful golden crust but are still slightly pink in the center. Remove to a plate and tent loosely with foil.

In the same skillet (don't clean it - those salmon bits are flavor!), reduce heat to medium and add the butter. Once melted, toss in the minced garlic and cook for about 30-60 seconds until it smells amazing but doesn't brown. Pour in the heavy cream, stirring constantly and scraping up any browned bits from the bottom of the pan. Let it bubble gently for about 2 minutes, then whisk in the parmesan until melted. Add the salt, black pepper, red pepper flakes if using, and lemon juice. Taste and adjust seasonings.

Add the drained pasta directly to the sauce and toss well to coat every strand. If the sauce seems too thick, add a splash of that reserved pasta water to loosen it up. Let the pasta hang out in the sauce for a minute to absorb some of that flavor.

Twirl that creamy pasta onto warmed plates, nestle the crispy salmon pieces on top, and shower everything with chopped parsley and a little extra grated parmesan if you're feeling fancy. Best enjoyed immediately with someone you really like.

attrs.heading: If something goes wrong
attrs.intro: A few of the more common issues, and what tends to fix them.

items.symptom: Sauce won't cling to the pasta
items.cause: Pasta drained too thoroughly, or sauce too thin.
items.fix: Reserve a ladle of starchy cooking water before draining. Toss it through with the pasta and sauce in the pan over heat — the starch binds everything.

items.cause: Overcooked, or sat in water after draining.
items.fix: Cook a minute less than the packet says, drain, and finish in the sauce. Time it so the pasta meets the sauce in the pan, not the colander.

items.cause: Pan too hot, or cheese added all at once.
items.fix: Lower the heat first. Add the cheese a small handful at a time, stirring continuously, with a splash of pasta water to keep the sauce loose.

items.cause: Tomato or wine hasn't cooked down long enough.
items.fix: Give the sauce another ten to fifteen minutes at a low simmer. A pinch of sugar softens an acidic tomato base.

content.text: Vegetarian: drop the meat and add 200 g mushrooms (chestnut or porcini) sautéed until they squeak.
Lighter sauce: replace cream with crème fraîche stirred in off the heat — same richness, less weight.
The sauce reheats well the next day; cook the pasta fresh.
A British home cook's dish. It sits among the recipes families remember without thinking about — the kind that gets adjusted across generations and never quite the same twice.
